content.full_text:
State of California

Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 907 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive Legal • • Enforcement • • State-ments of Economic Interests
916 322-5660        322-5660           322-5901         322-5441         322-0444


December 20, 1983


Jorge Leon
Deputy Executive Secretary
Agricultural Labor Relations Board
916 Capitol Mall, Room 313
Sacramento, CA 95814

RE: Your Request for Advice
     Car No. A-83-1-273

Dear Mr. Leon:

You have written requesting our advice regarding the "revolving door" statute contained in the Political Reform Act (the "Act"). Your agency has received a complaint that a former employee of the agency has been hired by a party to a quasi-judicial proceeding before the agency. The complaint alleges that the former state employee has been participating in representing the party in a proceeding in which the employee previously participated as an investigator for your agency.

Your agency is being asked to entertain a petition that the former employee be excluded from further participation in the proceeding pursuant to section 87404. You are seeking the Commission's advice relative to the question of who is the appropriate officer to resolve such a question. Your agency's interpretation and the facts upon which it is based have been stated in your letter as follows:

Our reading of Section 87404 is that it authorizes the Agricultural Labor Relations Board (ALRB), as a quasi-judicial body, to consider the complaint, hold an

1/ Government Code Sections 81000-91014. The "revolving door" statute is found at Sections 87400-87405. All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise specified.

Jorge Leon
December 27, 1985
Page 2

administrative hearing thereon, and, if the evidence
supports such a conclusion, find that the ex-employee
is prohibited from participating in the case and
exclude him from further participation. However, we
would appreciate your opinion on this interpretation.

The ALRB was created in 1975 to enforce the
Agricultural Labor Relations Act, Labor Code section
1140, et. seq. Although not subject to the
Administrative Procedures Act (Government Code section
11500, et. seq.), the ALRB is a "quasi-judicial"
agency, empowered to issue complaints on charges of
unfair labor practices, to hold hearings thereon before
appointed hearing officers, to make findings of fact
and conclusions of law, and to render decisions and
issue remedial orders. Such hearings are conducted
pursuant to the Evidence Code, and final decisions of
the Board are reviewable by petition for writ of review
in the courts of appeal. (See Labor Code sections
1160.2, 1160.3 and 1160.3.1.) The agency's proceedings
would thus appear to fit the definition of
"quasi-judicial or other proceedings" set forth in
Government Code section 3100(c).

CONCLUSION

We agree with your reading of the provisions of section
3764, which states as follows:

Upon the petition of any interested person or
party, the court or the presiding of other officer,
including but not limited to a hearing officer serving
pursuant to section 11512 of the Government Code, in
any judicial, quasi-judicial or other proceeding,
including but not limited to any proceeding pursuant to
Chapter 5 (commencing with section 11500) of Part 1 of
Division 3 of Title 2 of the Government Code may, after
notice and an opportunity for a hearing, exclude any
person found to be in violation of this article from
further participation, or from assisting or counseling
any other participant, in the proceeding then pending
before such court or presiding or other officer

(Emphasis added.)

Under the facts set forth in your letter, the "presiding or
other officer" in this proceeding is the appointed hearing
officer serving in the quasi-judicial proceeding. This is the

Jorge Leon
December 20, 1963
Page 3


appropriate official to hear and determine whether exclusion is
appropriate.

The intent of Section d7404 appears to be that the normal
quasi-judicial procedures of the agency in question should be
followed when a participant in the proceeding is challenged.
Where the proceedings are conducted pursuant to the
Administrative Procedure Act, the appropriate officer is the
hearing officer who hears motions or similar proceedings, as
assigned by the Office of Administrative Hearings. Your agency
has its own specific procedural rules and hearing officers.
Consequently, within that structure, the appropriate officer
would be the person or persons who perform the analogous
functions within your procedures. In this case, the hearing
officer assigned to this proceeding would therefore be the
appropriate officer.

In rendering this advice we in no way imply any opinion as
to the proper ruling on the substantive issue of exclusion. We
have not been presented with the facts in the matter and our
advice on this issue has not been requested. Section 83114(b).

Should you have questions regarding this letter, I may be
reached at (916) 322-5901.

Sincerely,

[illegible] Milman
Barbara A. Milman
General Counsel

BAM:pln

STATE OF CALIFORNIA                                    GEORGE DEUKMEJIAN, Governor

AGRICULTURAL LABOR RELATIONS BOARD
915 Capitol Mall, Room 335
Sacramento, Ca 95814
(916) 322-6612 6913


December 9, 1983


Mr. Robert Leidigh
Fair Political Practices Commission
Post Office Box 807
1100 K Street
Sacramento, CA 95804

                Re: Government Code Section 87400, et. seq.

Dear Mr. Leidigh:

     This is a request for advice concerning enforcement of
Government Code section 87400.

     We have received a complaint that a former employee of this
agency has been hired by a respondent in an unfair labor
practice proceeding now pending before the Board and has
been participating in representing the respondent in said
proceeding. The complaint alleges that the former employee,
who worked for us as an investigator, is now assisting the
respondent in the investigation of cases he was assigned to
while he worked for our agency.

     Government Code section 87401 provides as follows:

          No former state administrative official, after
          the termination of his or her employment or
          term of office, shall for compensation act as
          agent or attorney for, or otherwise represent,
          any other person (other than the State of
          California) before the court or state admini-
          strative agency or any officer or employee
          thereof;

               (a) By making any formal or informal appear-
                   ance, or by making any oral or written
                   communication with the intent to influence,
                   in connection with any judicial, quasi-
                   judicial or other proceeding if;

               (b) The State of California is a party or has
                   a direct or substantial interest; and

               (c) If the proceeding is one in which the former
                   state administrative official participated.

Mr. Robert Leidigh                    - 2 -                    December 9, 1983


Government Code section 87404 provides that:

       Upon the petition of any interested person or
       party, the court or presiding or other
       officer, including but not limited to a hearing
       officer serving pursuant to Section 11512 of
       the Government Code, in any judicial, quasi-
       judicial or other proceeding, including but
       not limited to any proceeding pursuant to
       Chapter 5 (commencing with Section 11500) of
       Part 1 of Division 3 of Title 2 of the Government
       Code may, after notice and an opportunity for a
       hearing, exclude any person found to be in
       violation of this article from further partic-
       ipation, or from assisting or counseling any
       other participant, in the proceeding then
       pending before such court or presiding or other
       officer.

Our reading of section 87404 is that it authorizes the
Agricultural Labor Relations Board (ALRB), as a quasi-judicial
body, to consider the complaint, hold an administrative
hearing thereon, and, if the evidence supports such a con-
clusion, find that the ex-employee is prohibited from
participating in the case and exclude him from further
participation. However, we would appreciate your opinion
on this interpretation.

The ALRB was created in 1975 to enforce the Agricultural
Labor Relations Act, Labor Code section 1140, et. seq.
Although not subject to the Administrative Procedures Act
(Government Code section 11500, et. seq.), the ALRB is a
"quasi-judicial" agency, empowered to issue complaints on
charges of unfair labor practices, to hold hearings thereon
before appointed hearing officers, to make findings of fact
and conclusions of law, and to render decisions and issue
remedial orders. Such hearings are conducted pursuant to the
Evidence Code, and final decisions of the Board are reviewable
by petition for writ of review in the courts of appeal. (See
Labor Code sections 1160.2, 1160.3 and 1160.8.) The agency's
proceedings would thus appear to fit the definition of "quasi-
judicial or other proceedings" set forth in Government Code
87400(c).

We would appreciate your earliest possible attention to this
matter, as a pre-hearing conference in the unfair labor pro-
ceeding in question is scheduled for December 19, 1983.

Sincerely,

[signature]
GEORGE LEON
Deputy Executive Secretary

cc: Robert Blasier, Jr.

sections.conclusion:
We agree with your reading of the provisions of Section 87404, which states as follows:

Upon the petition of any interested person or party, the court or the presiding or other officer, including but not limited to a hearing officer serving pursuant to Section 11512 of the Government Code, in any judicial, quasi-judicial or other proceeding, including but not limited to any proceeding pursuant to Chapter 5 (commencing with Section 11500) of Part 1 of Division 3 of Title 2 of the Government Code may, after notice and an opportunity for a hearing, exclude any person found to be in violation of this article from further participation, or from assisting or counseling any other participant, in the proceeding then pending before such court or presiding or other officer

(Emphasis added.)

Under the facts set forth in your letter, the "presiding or other officer" in this proceeding is the appointed hearing officer serving in the quasi-judicial proceeding. This is the

appropriate official to hear and determine whether exclusion is appropriate.

The intent of Section 87404 appears to be that the normal quasi-judicial procedures of the agency in question should be followed when a participant in the proceeding is challenged. Where the proceedings are conducted pursuant to the Administrative Procedure Act, the appropriate officer is the hearing officer who hears motions or similar proceedings, as assigned by the Office of Administrative Hearings. Your agency has its own specific procedural rules and hearing officers. Consequently, within that structure, the appropriate officer would be the person or persons who perform the analogous functions within your procedures. In this case, the hearing officer assigned to this proceeding would therefore be the appropriate officer.

In rendering this advice we in no way imply any opinion as to the proper ruling on the substantive issue of exclusion. We have not been presented with the facts in the matter and our advice on this issue has not been requested. Section 83114(b).
